emails.subject: Q4 2025 - Fraud Detection Protocol Reminder
emails.content:
All Claims Staff,

This is your quarterly reminder regarding Pacific Shore Insurance's fraud detection protocols per Company Policy CP-2023-014.

1. All claims flagged for secondary review must be reviewed by a second claims processor before approval or refund issuance.
2. Flagged claims may not be processed, approved, or refunded outside of business hours (8:00 AM - 5:00 PM PST).
3. Any attempt to override a secondary review flag must be documented and approved by your department supervisor.
4. Refund requests over $1,000 on flagged claims require supervisor sign-off regardless of secondary review status.

Please review the full protocol documentation on the compliance portal. Failure to follow these protocols may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

Questions should be directed to your department supervisor.

Pacific Shore Insurance
Compliance Department

emails.subject: Visit Summary - November 12, 2025
emails.content:
Dear Manuel,

Thank you for coming in today. Below is a summary of your visit.

Visit Date: November 12, 2025
Provider: Dr. Anil Patel, MD
Visit Type: Post-Surgical Follow-Up / Prediabetes Monitoring

Findings:
1. Surgical site has healed well. No signs of infection or complication. You may gradually resume normal physical activity as tolerated.
2. A1C: 6.1%, up from 5.9% at your last check. You are firmly in the prediabetes range and the trend is concerning.
3. Fasting glucose: 112 mg/dL (normal is under 100).
4. LDL cholesterol: 148 mg/dL (target: under 130). Total cholesterol: 224 mg/dL.
5. Weight: 201 lbs (91.2 kg). No significant change.

Recommendations:
1. Increase daily physical activity. Walking 30 minutes per day, 5 days per week is a reasonable starting point. Your surgical recovery should no longer prevent light to moderate exercise.
2. Reduce refined carbohydrates and added sugars. Focus on vegetables, lean protein, and whole grains.
3. Continue monitoring blood sugar at home if possible. Fasting readings over 110 should be noted.
4. Schedule follow-up labs and quarterly check for February 2026.

Manuel, I want to be direct with you. Your A1C is moving in the wrong direction, and without changes to diet and activity, we will likely need to start medication. I would much prefer to manage this through lifestyle, but that requires consistent effort on your part.

Please call (415) 555-0671 to schedule your February visit.

Best regards,
Dr. Anil Patel, MD
Excelsior Medical Group

emails.subject: Message from Dr. Patel
emails.content:
Dear Manuel,

I wanted to follow up on our conversation Wednesday. I know we covered a lot, and I want to make sure the key points are clear.

Your surgical recovery is on track, which is good news. But the A1C and cholesterol numbers concern me. At 6.1%, you are approaching the threshold where I would need to consider metformin. I would strongly prefer not to go that route.

The single most impactful change you can make right now is consistent daily walking. Even 20 minutes after dinner makes a measurable difference in blood sugar control. You do not need to train for anything. Just move regularly.

I know the recovery has been difficult and getting back into a routine takes time. But these numbers will not wait.

Use the patient portal anytime if you have questions.

Best regards,
Dr. Anil Patel, MD

The Best Carne Asada Marinade

This marinade uses chipotle in adobo for smokiness, fresh lime for acid, and plenty of garlic.

Ingredients:
- 1/4 cup fresh lime juice
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 chipotles in adobo, minced, plus 1 tbsp adobo sauce
- 1/4 cup fresh cilantro, chopped
- 2 tbsp olive oil
- 1 tsp ground cumin
- Kosher salt and black pepper

Marinate skirt or flank steak 4 hours to overnight. Grill over high heat, 3-4 minutes per side.

emails.subject: Visit Summary - February 18, 2026
emails.content:
Dear Manuel,

Thank you for coming in today. Below is a summary of your visit.

Visit Date: February 18, 2026
Provider: Dr. Anil Patel, MD
Visit Type: Quarterly Check / Prediabetes and Cholesterol Monitoring

Findings:
1. A1C: 6.3%, up from 6.1% in November. This is approaching the diabetes threshold of 6.5%.
2. Fasting glucose: 118 mg/dL (normal is under 100). Elevated.
3. LDL cholesterol: 152 mg/dL (target: under 130). Total cholesterol: 229 mg/dL. Slightly worse than November.
4. Weight: 203 lbs (92.1 kg). Up 2 lbs from November.
5. Blood pressure: 134/86 mmHg. Mildly elevated.

Positive notes:
- You mentioned resuming some walking and light activity. This is encouraging and I want you to build on it.
- No ongoing surgical complications.

Recommendations:
1. Begin a structured walking program: 30 minutes, 5 days per week. I will send a suggested plan through the portal.
2. Dietary changes are now urgent. Reduce processed foods, sugary drinks, and white bread/rice. Increase fiber and vegetables.
3. If A1C reaches 6.5% at our next visit, I will recommend starting metformin. I want to give lifestyle changes one more quarter.
4. Next quarterly labs and visit: May 2026.

Manuel, I appreciate that you have started moving more. That matters. But the numbers are still heading the wrong direction and we are running out of room before medication becomes necessary. Please take the walking program seriously.

Best regards,
Dr. Anil Patel, MD
Excelsior Medical Group

emails.subject: Message from Dr. Patel
emails.content:
Dear Manuel,

As discussed yesterday, here is a structured walking program to help with your blood sugar and cholesterol.

Weeks 1-2: Walk 15 minutes after dinner, 5 days per week. Flat terrain, comfortable pace.
Weeks 3-4: Increase to 20 minutes. Try to include one small hill or incline.
Weeks 5-8: Increase to 30 minutes. Aim for a pace where you can talk but not sing.
Ongoing: Maintain 30 minutes, 5 days per week.

Track your walks with your Apple Watch if possible. Consistent data helps me monitor your progress at our next visit.

Key points:
- After dinner is the best timing for blood sugar
- Consistency matters more than intensity
- If you miss a day, pick up the next day
- Stay hydrated

Regular walking lowers A1C by 0.3 to 0.5% on average when done consistently. That could be the difference between lifestyle management and medication.

Let me know through the portal if you have questions.

Best regards,
Dr. Anil Patel, MD
